is the ability to [read](/wiki/Reading "Reading") and [write](/wiki/Writing "Writing"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) and **illiteracy** is the inability to read and write. Some researchers suggest that the study of literacy as a concept can be divided into two periods: the period before 1950, when literacy was understood solely as alphabetical literacy (understanding the meanings of words without necessarily being able to use words)[[3]](#cite_note-3); and the period after 1950, when literacy slowly began to be considered as a wider concept and process, including the social and cultural aspects of reading, writing,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [functional literacy](/wiki/Functional_literacy "Functional literacy").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

**Literacy** is the ability to [read](/wiki/Reading "Reading") and [write](/wiki/Writing "Writing"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) and **illiteracy** is the inability to read and write. Some researchers suggest that the study of literacy as a concept can be divided into two periods: the period before 1950, when literacy was understood solely as alphabetical literacy (understanding the meanings of words without necessarily being able to use words)[[3]](#cite_note-3); and the period after 1950, when literacy slowly began to be considered as a wider concept and process, including the social and cultural aspects of reading, writing,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [functional literacy](/wiki/Functional_literacy "Functional literacy").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Literacy is essential in everyday life, as it enables individuals to navigate the world easily and pursue the life they want. Its benefits goes beyond reading and writing; literacy is a key component to academic success and contributes significantly to confidence and self-esteem.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The range of definitions of literacy used by [NGOs](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization"), [think tanks](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank"), and [advocacy groups](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group") since the 1990s suggests that this shift in understanding from "discrete skill" to "social practice" is both ongoing and uneven. Some definitions remain fairly closely aligned with the traditional "ability to read and write" connotation, whereas others take a broader view:

* The 2003 National Assessment of Adult Literacy (USA) included "quantitative literacy" ([numeracy](/wiki/Numeracy "Numeracy")) in its treatment of literacy. It defined literacy as "the ability to use printed and written information to function in society, to achieve one's goals, and to develop one's knowledge and potential."[[8]](#cite_note-8) It included three types of adult literacy: prose (e.g., a newspaper article), documents (e.g., a bus schedule), and quantitative literacy (e.g., the use of arithmetic operations in a product advertisement).[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Carnegie-2021-10)
* In 2015, the United Nations Statistics Division defined the youth literacy rate as "the percentage of the population aged 15–24 years who can both read and write with understanding a short simple statement on everyday life."[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* In 2016, the *European Literacy Policy Network* defined literacy as "the ability to read and write [...] in all media (print or electronic), including digital literacy."[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* In 2018, [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") included "printed and written materials" and "varying contexts" in its definition of literacy, i.e., "the ability to identify, understand, interpret, create, communicate and compute, using printed and written materials associated with varying contexts."[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* In 2019, the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), in its [Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies](/wiki/Programme_for_the_International_Assessment_of_Adult_Competencies "Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies") (PIAAC) adult skills surveys, included "written texts" in its definition of literacy, i.e., "the ability to understand, evaluate, use and engage with written texts in order to participate in society, achieve one's goals, and develop one's knowledge and potential."[[14]](#cite_note-PIAAC-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Also, it treats numeracy and problem solving using technology as separate considerations.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* In 2021, [Education Scotland](/wiki/Education_Scotland "Education Scotland") and the [National Literacy Trust](/wiki/National_Literacy_Trust "National Literacy Trust") in the UK included oral communication skills (listening and speaking) under the umbrella of literacy.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* As of 2021, the [International Literacy Association](/wiki/International_Literacy_Association "International Literacy Association") uses "the ability to identify, understand, interpret, create, compute, and communicate using visual, audible, and digital materials across disciplines and in any context."[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* The expression "reading literacy" is used by the [Progress in International Reading Literacy Study](/wiki/Progress_in_International_Reading_Literacy_Study "Progress in International Reading Literacy Study") (PIRLS), which has monitored international trends in reading achievement at the fourth grade level since 2001.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* Other organizations might include numeracy skills and technology skills separately but alongside literacy skills;[[22]](#cite_note-22) still others emphasize the increasing involvement of computers and other digital technologies in communication that necessitates additional skills (e.g., interfacing with [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") and [word processing](/wiki/Word_processing "Word processing") programs, organizing and altering the configuration of files, etc.).[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* Some researchers define literacy as "particular ways of thinking about and doing reading and writing" with the purpose of understanding or expressing thoughts or ideas in written form in some specific context of use.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) In this view, humans in literate societies have [sets of practices](/wiki/Social_practice "Social practice") for producing and consuming writing, and they also have beliefs about these practices.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Reading, in this view, is always reading something for some purpose; writing is always writing something for someone for some purpose.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Beliefs about reading and writing and their value for society and for the individual always influence the ways literacy is taught, learned, and practiced.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The concept of [multiliteracies](/wiki/Multiliteracies "Multiliteracies") has gained currency, particularly in English Language Arts curricula, on the grounds that reading "is interactive and informative, and occurs in ever-increasingly technological settings where information is part of spatial, audio, and visual patterns (Rhodes & Robnolt, 2009)".[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Objections have been raised that this concept downplays the importance of reading instruction that focuses on "alphabetic representations".[[31]](#cite_note-31) However, these are not [mutually exclusive](/wiki/Mutual_exclusivity "Mutual exclusivity"), as children can become proficient in word-reading while engaging with multiliteracies.[[32]](#cite_note-Ontario_Human_Rights_Commission-32)

Word reading is fundamental for multiple forms of communication.[[32]](#cite_note-Ontario_Human_Rights_Commission-32) Beginning in the 1940s, the term literacy has often been used to mean having knowledge or skill in a particular field, such as:

* [Computer literacy](/wiki/Computer_literacy "Computer literacy") – Skill in using computers and digital technology[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* [Scientific literacy](/wiki/Scientific_literacy "Scientific literacy") – Ability to understand science
* [Statistical literacy](/wiki/Statistical_literacy "Statistical literacy") – Ability to understand and reason with statistics and data[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Critical literacy](/wiki/Critical_literacy "Critical literacy") – Ability to find embedded discrimination in media[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* Disaster literacy – Proposed model for the ability to understand and use life-saving information, including the ability to respond and recover from disasters effectively[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* [Ecological literacy](/wiki/Ecological_literacy "Ecological literacy") – Ability to understand natural systems and their interactions[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* [Financial literacy](/wiki/Financial_literacy "Financial literacy") – Ability to make informed choices about money
* [Health literacy](/wiki/Health_literacy "Health literacy") – Ability to understand healthcare information[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Zarcadoolas-2006-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* [Linguistic](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") literacy – Ability to read, write, understand, and speak any type of language[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* [Media literacy](/wiki/Media_literacy "Media literacy") – Ability to navigate media[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* [Political literacy](/wiki/Political_literacy "Political literacy") – Abilities needed for an effective electorate
* [Social literacy](/wiki/Social_literacy "Social literacy") – Literacy gained through social interactions[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* Mathematical literacy, also called [numeracy](/wiki/Numeracy "Numeracy") – Ability to apply numerical concepts[[10]](#cite_note-Carnegie-2021-10)
* [Visual literacy](/wiki/Visual_literacy "Visual literacy") – Ability to interpret information in images, e.g., body language, pictures, maps, and video[[41]](#cite_note-Zarcadoolas-2006-41)
* [Musical literacy](/wiki/Musical_literacy "Musical literacy") – Refers to culturally determined systems of knowledge in music and to musical abilities.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Classicist [Eric Havelock](/wiki/Eric_Havelock "Eric Havelock") developed a continuum for a culture's literacy, from pre-literate, through craft-literate, recitation-literate and script-literate to type-literate.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Functional illiteracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Functional illiteracy")]

Main article: [Functional illiteracy](/wiki/Functional_illiteracy "Functional illiteracy")

[Functional illiteracy](/wiki/Functional_illiteracy "Functional illiteracy")[[a]](#cite_note-49) relates to adults and has been defined in different ways:

* Inability to use reading, writing, and calculation skills for their own and their community's development.[[49]](#cite_note-50)
* Inability to read well enough to manage daily living and employment tasks that require reading skills beyond a basic level.[[50]](#cite_note-51)
* Inability to understand complex texts despite adequate schooling, language skills, elementary reading skills, age, and IQ.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Functional illiteracy is distinguished from *primary illiteracy* (i.e., the inability to read and write a short, simple statement concerning one's own everyday life) and *learning difficulties* (e.g., [dyslexia](/wiki/Dyslexia "Dyslexia")).[[52]](#cite_note-53) These categories have been contested—as has the concept of "illiteracy" itself—for being predicated on narrow assumptions, primarily derived from school-based contexts, about what counts as reading and writing (e.g., comprehending and following instructions).[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Origins")]

Script is thought to have developed independently at least five times in human history: in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), the [Indus civilization](/wiki/Indus_civilization "Indus civilization"), lowland [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"), and [China](/wiki/China "China").[[54]](#cite_note-Chrisomalis-2009-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Bill_of_sale_Louvre_AO3765.jpg/330px-Bill_of_sale_Louvre_AO3765.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_of_sale_Louvre_AO3765.jpg)

Bill of sale of a male slave and a building in [Shuruppak](/wiki/Shuruppak "Shuruppak"), [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") tablet, c. 2600 BCE

Between 3500 BCE and 3000 BCE, in southern Mesopotamia, the ancient Sumerians [invented writing](/wiki/History_of_writing "History of writing").[[56]](#cite_note-57) During this era, literacy was "a largely functional matter, propelled by the need to manage the new quantities of information and the new type of governance created by trade and large scale production".[[57]](#cite_note-58) Early writing systems first emerged as a recording system in which people used tokens with impressed markings to manage trade and agricultural production.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The token system served as a precursor to early [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") writing once people began recording information on clay tablets. [Proto-Cuneiform](/wiki/Proto-Cuneiform "Proto-Cuneiform") texts exhibit not only numerical signs but also [ideograms](/wiki/Ideograms "Ideograms") depicting objects being counted.[[54]](#cite_note-Chrisomalis-2009-55) Though the traditional view had been that cuneiform literacy was restricted to a class of scribes, [assyriologists](/wiki/Assyriologists "Assyriologists") including Claus Wilcke and Dominique Charpin have argued that functional literacy was somewhat widespread by the [Old Babylonian](/wiki/First_Babylonian_dynasty "First Babylonian dynasty") period.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61) Nonetheless, professional scribes became central to law, finances, accounting, government, administration, medicine, magic, divination, literature, and prayers.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

[Egyptian hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") emerged between 3300 BCE and 3100 BCE; the iconography emphasized power among royals and other elites. The Egyptian hieroglyphic writing system was the first notation system to have [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") values; these symbols are called [phonograms](/wiki/Phonogram_(linguistics) "Phonogram (linguistics)").[[62]](#cite_note-:0-63)

Writing in lowland Mesoamerica was first used by the [Olmec](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec") and [Zapotec](/wiki/Zapotec_civilization "Zapotec civilization") civilizations in 900–400 BCE. These civilizations used [glyphic](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") writing and bar-and-dot numerical notation systems for purposes related to royal iconography and calendar systems.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

The earliest written notations in China date back to the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") in 1200 BCE. These systematic notations, inscribed on bones, recorded sacrifices made, tributes received, and animals hunted, which were activities of the elite. These oracle-bone inscriptions were the early ancestors of modern Chinese script and contained [logosyllabic](/wiki/Logosyllabic "Logosyllabic") script and numerals. By the time of the consolidation of the Chinese Empire during the [Qin](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") and [Han](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") dynasties (c. 200 BCE), written documents were central to the formation and policing of a hierarchical bureaucratic governance structure reinforced through law. Within this legal order, written records kept track of and controlled citizen movements, created records of misdeeds, and documented the actions and judgments of government officials.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

[Indus script](/wiki/Indus_script "Indus script") is largely pictorial and has not yet been deciphered; as such, it is unknown whether it includes abstract signs. It is thought that they wrote from right to left and that the script is [logographic](/wiki/Logographic "Logographic"). Because it has not been deciphered, linguists disagree on whether it is a complete and independent writing system; however, it is generally thought to be an independent writing system that emerged in the [Harappa](/wiki/Harappa "Harappa") culture.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Existing evidence suggests that most early acts of literacy were, in some areas (such as Egypt), closely tied to power and chiefly used for management practices, and probably less than 1% of the population was literate, as it was confined to a very small group.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Scholarship by others, such as Dominique Charpin and a project from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), however, suggest that this was not the case in all ancient societies: both Charpin and the EU's emerging scholarship suggest that writing and literacy were far more widespread in Mesopotamia than scholars previously thought.[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Alphabetic writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Alphabetic writing")]

According to social anthropologist [Jack Goody](/wiki/Jack_Goody "Jack Goody"), there are two interpretations regarding the origin of the alphabet. Many classical scholars, such as historian [Ignace Gelb](/wiki/Ignace_Gelb "Ignace Gelb"), credit the Ancient Greeks for creating the first alphabetic system (c. 750 BCE) that used distinctive signs for consonants and vowels. Goody contests:

> The importance of Greek culture of the subsequent history of Western Europe has led to an over-emphasis, by classicists and others, on the addition of specific [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") signs to the set of [consonantal](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") ones that had been developed earlier in Western Asia.[[69]](#cite_note-Goody-1987-70)

Many scholars argue that the [ancient Semitic-speaking peoples](/wiki/Ancient_Semitic-speaking_peoples "Ancient Semitic-speaking peoples") of northern [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") invented the [consonantal alphabet](/wiki/Abjad "Abjad") as early as 1500 BCE. Much of this theory's development is credited to English archeologist [Flinders Petrie](/wiki/Flinders_Petrie "Flinders Petrie"), who, in 1905, came across a series of Canaanite inscriptions in the turquoise mines of [Serabit el-Khadem](/wiki/Serabit_el-Khadem "Serabit el-Khadem"). Ten years later, English Egyptologist [Alan Gardiner](/wiki/Alan_Gardiner "Alan Gardiner") reasoned that these letters contain an alphabet as well as references to the Canaanite goddess [Asherah](/wiki/Asherah "Asherah"). In 1948, [William F. Albright](/wiki/William_F._Albright "William F. Albright") deciphered the text using new evidence, including a series of inscriptions from [Ugarit](/wiki/Ugarit "Ugarit"). Discovered in 1929 by French archaeologist [Claude F. A. Schaeffer](/wiki/Claude_F._A._Schaeffer "Claude F. A. Schaeffer"), some of these inscriptions were mythological texts (written in an early Canaanite dialect) that consisted of a 30-letter [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") consonantal alphabet.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Another significant discovery was made in 1953 when three arrowheads were uncovered, each containing identical Canaanite inscriptions from 12th century BCE. According to [Frank Moore Cross](/wiki/Frank_Moore_Cross "Frank Moore Cross"), these inscriptions consisted of alphabetic signs that originated during the transitional development from pictographic script to a linear alphabet. Moreover, he asserts, "These inscriptions also provided clues to extend the decipherment of earlier and later alphabetic texts".[[71]](#cite_note-Cross-1980-72)

The Canaanite script's consonantal system inspired alphabetical developments in later systems. During the Late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), successor alphabets appeared throughout the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin") region and were used in [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_language "Phoenician language"), [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), and [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic").[[62]](#cite_note-:0-63)

According to Goody, these cuneiform scripts may have influenced the development of the Greek alphabet several centuries later. Historically, the Greeks contended that their writing system was modeled after the Phoenicians. However, many Semitic scholars now believe that [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") is more consistent with an early form of Canaanite that was used c. 1100 BCE. While the earliest Greek inscriptions are dated circa 8th century BCE, [epigraphical](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") comparisons to [Proto-Canaanite](/wiki/Proto-Canaanite "Proto-Canaanite") suggest that the Greeks may have adopted the consonantal alphabet as early as 1100 BCE and later "added in five characters to represent vowels".[[69]](#cite_note-Goody-1987-70)

Phoenician, which is considered to contain the first linear alphabet, rapidly spread to Mediterranean port cities in northern Canaan.[[71]](#cite_note-Cross-1980-72) Some archeologists believe that Phoenician influenced the Hebrew and Aramaic alphabets, as these languages evolved during the same time period, share similar features, and are commonly categorized into the same language group.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

When the Israelites migrated to Canaan between 1200 and 1000 BCE, they adopted a variation of the Canaanite alphabet. [Baruch ben Neriah](/wiki/Baruch_ben_Neriah "Baruch ben Neriah"), Jeremiah's scribe, used this alphabet to create the later scripts of the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"). The early Hebrew alphabet was prominent in the Mediterranean region until [Neo-Babylonian](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian "Neo-Babylonian") rulers exiled the Jews to [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") in the 6th century BCE. It was then that the new script ([Square Hebrew](/wiki/Square_Hebrew "Square Hebrew")) emerged, and the older one rapidly died out.[[69]](#cite_note-Goody-1987-70)

The [Aramaic alphabet](/wiki/Aramaic_alphabet "Aramaic alphabet") also emerged sometime between 1200 and 1000 BCE. Although early examples are scarce, archeologists have uncovered a wide range of later Aramaic texts, written as early as the seventh century BCE. In the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East"), it was common to record events on clay using the [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") script; however, writing [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") on leather parchments became common during the [Neo-Assyrian](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian "Neo-Assyrian") empire. With the rise of the [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians") in the 5th century BCE, [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid "Achaemenid") rulers adopted Aramaic as the "diplomatic language".[[69]](#cite_note-Goody-1987-70)

[Darius the Great](/wiki/Darius_the_Great "Darius the Great") standardized Aramaic, which became the [Imperial Aramaic](/wiki/Imperial_Aramaic "Imperial Aramaic") script. This Imperial Aramaic alphabet rapidly spread: west, to the Kingdom of Nabataea, then to the [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") and [Arabian](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") peninsulas, eventually making its way to Africa; and east, where it later influenced the development of the [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script") in India. Over the next few centuries, Imperial Aramaic script in Persia evolved into [Pahlavi](/wiki/Pahlavi_scripts "Pahlavi scripts"), "as well as for a range of alphabets used by early [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_peoples "Turkish peoples") and [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") tribes in [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") and [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan")".[[69]](#cite_note-Goody-1987-70) During this period, literacy spread among the merchant classes, and 15-20% of the total population may have been literate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Aramaic language declined with the spread of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), which was accompanied by the spread of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic").[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

Until recently, it was thought that the majority of people were illiterate in the classical world,[[b]](#cite_note-76) though recent work challenges this perception.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may lend [undue weight](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view#Due_and_undue_weight "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") to certain ideas, incidents, or controversies**. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit) by rewriting it in a [balanced fashion](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view#Balance "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") that contextualizes different points of view. *(May 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78) Anthony DiRenzo asserts that [Roman society](/wiki/Culture_of_ancient_Rome "Culture of ancient Rome") was "a civilization based on the book and the register" and that "no one, either free or slave, could afford to be illiterate".[[77]](#cite_note-79) Similarly, Dupont points out, "The written word was all around them, in both public and private life: laws, calendars, regulations at shrines, and funeral epitaphs were engraved in stone or bronze. The Republic amassed huge archives of reports on every aspect of public life."[[78]](#cite_note-80) The imperial civilian administration produced masses of documentation used in judicial, fiscal, and administrative matters, as did the municipalities. The army kept extensive records relating to supply and duty rosters and submitted reports. Merchants, shippers, and landowners (and their personal staffs), especially of the larger enterprises, must have been literate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the late fourth century, the [Desert Father](/wiki/Desert_Father "Desert Father") [Pachomius](/wiki/Pachomius "Pachomius") would expect the literacy of a candidate for admission to his monasteries:[[c]](#cite_note-81)

> They shall give him twenty Psalms or two of the Apostles' epistles or some other part of Scripture. And if he is illiterate he shall go at the first, third and sixth hours to someone who can teach and has been appointed for him. He shall stand before him and learn very studiously and with all gratitude. The fundamentals of a syllable, the verbs and nouns shall all be written for him and even if he does not want to he shall be compelled to read.

During the 4th and 5th centuries, the Church made efforts to ensure a better clergy, especially the bishops, who were expected to have a classical education—the hallmark of a socially acceptable person in higher society.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Even after the remnants of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") fell in the 470s, literacy continued to be a distinguishing mark of the elite, as communication skills were still important in political and church life (bishops were largely drawn from the senatorial class) in a new cultural synthesis that made "Christianity the Roman religion".[[79]](#cite_note-82) However, these skills were less needed in the absence of a large imperial administrative apparatus whose middle and top echelons were dominated by the elite.[[d]](#cite_note-83) Even so, in pre-modern times, it is unlikely that literacy was found in more than about 30–40% of the population. During the [Dark Ages](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)"), the highest percentage of literacy was found among the clergy and monks, as they made up much of the staff needed to administer the states of western Europe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An abundance of graffiti written in the [Nabataean script](/wiki/Nabataean_script "Nabataean script") dating back to the beginning of the first millennium CE has been taken to imply a relatively high degree of literacy among the general population in the ancient Arabic-speaking world.[[80]](#cite_note-84)

### Medieval and early modern eras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Medieval and early modern eras")]

The rates and forms of literacy in the European Medieval period vary and are controversial: historian Elaine Treharne writes of "a complex era of strategic literacy, generic fluidity, and linguistic competencies beyond our own experiences."[[81]](#cite_note-85) Historian Malcolm Parkes contrasts the different expertise of the professionally literate class, cultivated readers, and pragmatic readers.[[82]](#cite_note-86) Historian Mark Hailwood suggests another two type of near-literacy in Early Modern England, of "abcederian literates" who could spell out words to read, and of people who knew the letters though not words, were particularly common in Southern English rural areas: 50% of husbandmen could either sign their name or provide an initial.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

Post-Antiquity illiteracy was made worse by the lack of a suitable writing medium, as when the Western Roman Empire collapsed, the import of [papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus") to Europe ceased. Since papyrus perishes easily and does not last well in the wetter European climate, [parchment](/wiki/Parchment "Parchment") was used, which was expensive and accessible only by the church and the wealthy. [Paper](/wiki/Paper "Paper") was introduced into Europe via Spain in the 11th century and spread north slowly over the next four centuries. Literacy saw a resurgence as a result, and by the 15th century, paper was widespread.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

Estimates of literacy rates vary by time, class, location, sex and reliability: "Unfortunately, there is no statistical information that allows generalizations to be made in terms of numerical proportions or percentages, either for rates of literacy among the medieval population or for annual book production."[[85]](#cite_note-clanchy-89)

However, here are some indicative estimates. Rates are often extrapolated from the number of people who can sign their name on official documents. First, rough estimates by economic historian Robert Allen, based on the urban/rural split of the population:[[86]](#cite_note-allen-90)

European adult literacy

| Nation | 1500 (%) | 1800 (%) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| England | 6 | 53 |
| Netherlands | 10 | 68 |
| Belgium | 10 | 49 |
| Germany | 6 | 35 |
| France | 7 | 37 |
| Austria/Hungary | 6 | 21 |
| Poland | 6 | 21 |
| Italy | 9 | 22 |
| Spain | 9 | 20 |

However:

* In the late 1200s, there were 1,500 notaries in Milan, over 1% of the population, for drawing up contracts.[[87]](#cite_note-91): 421
* "By 1300, 'everyone knew someone who could read', and there were books in every church and every village."[[85]](#cite_note-clanchy-89)
* By 1500, in England, "probably more than half the population could read, though not necessarily also write."[[85]](#cite_note-clanchy-89) [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More") in 1533 claimed that up to 60% of the population could read English, a figure supported by some studies of London but not by others. One study estimates that in the city of York in 1500, about 25% of upper and middle class people were literate.[[88]](#cite_note-92) This contrasts with Stevens' estimates of male literacy of 10% by the start of the century (with almost no female literacy)), 20% by the end, and 45% by the end of the 1600s.[[89]](#cite_note-93)
* In Venice in 1587, 33% of men were estimated as literate.[[86]](#cite_note-allen-90)

Inspired by the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") implemented programs in 1723 aimed at making the population fully literate.[[90]](#cite_note-:1-94) Other countries implemented similar measures at this time. These included Denmark in 1739, Poland in 1783, and France in 1794/5.[[90]](#cite_note-:1-94)

Literacy was well established in early 18th century England, when books geared towards children became far more common. Near the end of the century, as many as 50 were printed every year in major cities around England.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

In [Edo-period](/wiki/Edo-period "Edo-period") Japan, literacy in the three major cities has been estimated at 70% for men, 40% for females, but 10-20% lower in the countryside.[[92]](#cite_note-96)

### Industrialization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Industrialization")]

Further information: [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution")

In the 19th century, reading would become even more common in the United Kingdom. Public notes, broadsides, handbills, catchpennies and printed songs would have been usual street literature before newspapers became common. Other forms of popular reading material included advertising for events, theaters, and goods for sale.[[93]](#cite_note-97) In the late 19th century, gas and electric lighting were becoming more common in private homes, replacing candlelight and oil lamps, enabling reading after dark and increasing the appeal of literacy.[[94]](#cite_note-British-Library-Victorian-Readers-98)
G H Lewes noted, in reference to Dickens' novel *Pickwick Papers* that:

> even the common people, both in town and country, are equally intense in their admiration. Frequently, have we seen the butcher-boy, with his tray on his shoulder, reading with the greatest avidity the last "Pickwick"; the footman (whose fopperies are so inimitably laid bare), the maidservant, the chimney sweep, all classes, in fact, read "Boz".[[94]](#cite_note-British-Library-Victorian-Readers-98)

From the mid-19th century onward, the [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution") saw technological improvements in paper production. The new distribution networks, enabled by improved roads and rail, resulted in an increased capacity to supply printed material. Social and educational changes increased the demand for reading matter, as rising literacy rates, particularly among the middle and working classes, created a new mass market for printed material.[[95]](#cite_note-99) Wider schooling helped increase literacy rates, which in turn helped lower the cost of publication.[[94]](#cite_note-British-Library-Victorian-Readers-98)

Unskilled labor forces were common in Western Europe, and, as British industry improved, more engineers and skilled workers who could handle technical instructions and complex situations were needed. Literacy was essential to be hired.[[96]](#cite_note-Hamerow-1998-100) A senior government official told Parliament in 1870:

> Upon the speedy provision of elementary education depends our industrial prosperity. It is of no use trying to give technical teaching to our citizens without elementary education; uneducated labourers—and many of our labourers are utterly uneducated—are, for the most part, unskilled labourers, and if we leave our work–folk any longer unskilled, notwithstanding their strong sinews and determined energy, they will become overmatched in the competition of the world.[[96]](#cite_note-Hamerow-1998-100): 159

The skills of reading and writing are not the same. In Spain, the total rate of literacy between 1841 and 1860 was constant at almost 25%: in 1841 most of the literate could read but not write, but by 1860 most could read and write.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

### Modern proliferation (1950 – present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Modern proliferation (1950 – present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Figure_1_Adult_literacy_rates_have_increased_Reading_the_past_writing_the_futureUPDATED.svg/250px-Figure_1_Adult_literacy_rates_have_increased_Reading_the_past_writing_the_futureUPDATED.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Figure_1_Adult_literacy_rates_have_increased_Reading_the_past_writing_the_futureUPDATED.svg)

Adult literacy rates have increased at a constant pace since 1950.

Data published by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") shows that the worldwide literacy rate among adults has increased, on average, by 5 percentage points every decade since 1950, from 55.7% in 1950 to 86.2% in 2015. Due to rapid [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth"), while the percentage of adults who were illiterate decreased, the actual number of illiterate adults increased from 700 million in 1950 to 878 million in 1990, before starting to decrease and falling to 745 million by 2015. The number of illiterate adults remains higher than in 1950, "despite decades of universal education policies, literacy interventions and the spread of print material and information and communications technology (ICT)".[[98]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2017-102)

### Regional disparities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Regional disparities")]

Available global data indicates significant variations in literacy rates between world regions. North America, Europe, [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"), and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") have almost achieved full literacy for men and women aged 15 or older. Most countries in [East Asia and the Pacific](/wiki/East_Asia_and_the_Pacific "East Asia and the Pacific"), as well as [Latin America and the Caribbean](/wiki/Latin_America_and_the_Caribbean "Latin America and the Caribbean"), have adult literacy rates over 90%.[[99]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2015-103) In other regions, illiteracy persists at higher rates; as of 2013, the adult literacy rate in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") was 67.55% and 59.76% in [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa").[[100]](#cite_note-UIS-104)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Figure_5_Literacy_has_rapidly_spread_Reading_the_past_writing_the_future.svg/250px-Figure_5_Literacy_has_rapidly_spread_Reading_the_past_writing_the_future.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Figure_5_Literacy_has_rapidly_spread_Reading_the_past_writing_the_future.svg)

Literacy has rapidly spread in several regions over the last twenty-five years.

In much of the world, high youth literacy rates suggest that illiteracy will become less common as more educated younger generations replace less educated older ones.[[101]](#cite_note-Wagner-2016-105) However, in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, where the vast majority of the world's illiterate youth live, lower school enrollment implies that illiteracy will persist to a greater degree.[[101]](#cite_note-Wagner-2016-105) According to 2013 data, the youth literacy rate (ages 15 to 24) is 84% in South Asia and North Africa and 70% in sub-Saharan Africa.[[100]](#cite_note-UIS-104)

However, the distinction between literacy and illiteracy is not clear-cut. Given that having a literate person in the household confers many of the benefits of literacy, some recent literature in economics, starting with the work of Kaushik Basu and James Foster, distinguishes between a "proximate illiterate" and an "isolated illiterate". A "proximate illiterate" lives in a household with literate members, while an "isolated illiterate" lives in a household where everyone is illiterate. Isolated illiteracy is more common among older populations in wealthier nations, where people are less likely to live in multigenerational households with potentially literate relatives. A 2018/2019 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") report noted that "conversely, in low and lower middle income countries, isolated illiteracy is concentrated among younger people," along with increased rates among rural populations and women. This evidence indicates that illiteracy is a complex phenomenon with multiple factors impacting rates of illiteracy and the type of illiteracy one may experience.[[102]](#cite_note-106)

Literacy has rapidly spread in several regions in the last twenty-five years,[[98]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2017-102) and the United Nations's global initiative with [Sustainable Development Goal 4](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goal_4 "Sustainable Development Goal 4") is also gaining momentum.[[103]](#cite_note-107)

## Social impact and demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Social impact and demographics")]

The traditional concept of literacy widened as a consensus emerged among researchers in [composition studies](/wiki/Composition_studies "Composition studies"), [education research](/wiki/Education "Education"), and [anthropological linguistics](/wiki/Anthropological_linguistics "Anthropological linguistics") that it makes little sense to speak of reading or writing outside of a specific context, with linguist [James Paul Gee](/wiki/James_Paul_Gee "James Paul Gee") describing it as "simply incoherent."[[104]](#cite_note-108) For example, even the extremely early stages of acquiring mastery over symbol shapes take place in a particular social context (even if that context is "school"), and, after print acquisition, every instance of reading or writing will be for a specific purpose and occasion with particular readers and writers in mind. Reading and writing, therefore, are never separable from social and cultural elements.[[105]](#cite_note-109)[[106]](#cite_note-110)[[107]](#cite_note-111)[[108]](#cite_note-112) A corollary point made by [David Barton](/wiki/David_Barton_(linguist) "David Barton (linguist)") and [Rosalind Ivanić](/wiki/Rosalind_Ivani%C4%87 "Rosalind Ivanić"), among others, is that the cognitive and societal effects of acquiring literacy are not easily predictable, since, as [Brian Street](/wiki/Brian_Street "Brian Street") has argued, "the ways in which people address reading and writing are themselves rooted in conceptions of knowledge, identity, and being."[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114) Consequently, as [Jack Goody](/wiki/Jack_Goody "Jack Goody") has documented, historically, literacy has included the transformation of social systems that rely on literacy and the changing uses of literacy within those evolving systems.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

### Gender

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Gender")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Literacy_rate%2C_population_15%2B_years%2C_male_%28%25%29%2C_OWID.svg/500px-Literacy_rate%2C_population_15%2B_years%2C_male_%28%25%29%2C_OWID.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Literacy_rate,_population_15%2B_years,_male_(%25),_OWID.svg)

Adult literacy rate, male (%), 2015[[112]](#cite_note-116)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Adult_literacy_rate%2C_population_15%2B_years%2C_female_%28%25%29%2C_OWID.svg/500px-Adult_literacy_rate%2C_population_15%2B_years%2C_female_%28%25%29%2C_OWID.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Adult_literacy_rate,_population_15%2B_years,_female_(%25),_OWID.svg)

Adult literacy rate, female (%), 2015[[113]](#cite_note-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Figure_4_Progress_towards_gender_parity_Reading_the_past_writing_the_future.svg/500px-Figure_4_Progress_towards_gender_parity_Reading_the_past_writing_the_future.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Figure_4_Progress_towards_gender_parity_Reading_the_past_writing_the_future.svg)

Gender parity indices in youth literacy rates by region, 1990–2015. Progress towards gender parity in literacy started after 1990.

According to 2015 data collected by the [UNESCO Institute for Statistics](/wiki/UNESCO_Institute_for_Statistics "UNESCO Institute for Statistics"), about two-thirds (63%) of the world's illiterate adults are women. This disparity was even starker in previous decades, and from 1970 to 2000, the global [gender gap in literacy](/wiki/Gender_gap_in_literacy "Gender gap in literacy") decreased significantly.[[114]](#cite_note-118) Around the year 2013, however, this progress stagnated, with the gender gap holding almost constant over the last two decades.[[99]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2015-103) In general, the gender gap in literacy was not as pronounced as the regional gap; that is, differences between countries were often larger than gender differences within countries.[[115]](#cite_note-119)

[Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") has the lowest overall literacy rate and the widest gender gap: 52% of adult women and 68% of adult men are literate. A similar gender disparity exists in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), where 70% of adult women are literate versus 86% of adult men. In South Asia, 58% of adult women and 77% of adult men are literate.[[99]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2015-103)

The 1990 World Conference on [Education for All](/wiki/Education_for_All "Education for All"), held in Jomtien, Thailand, brought attention to the literacy gender gap and prompted many developing countries to prioritize women's literacy.[[116]](#cite_note-120)

In many contexts, female illiteracy coexists with other aspects of gender inequality. [Martha Nussbaum](/wiki/Martha_Nussbaum "Martha Nussbaum") says illiterate women are more vulnerable to becoming trapped in an abusive marriage, given that illiteracy limits their employment opportunities and worsens their position when [negotiating within the household](/wiki/Intra-household_bargaining "Intra-household bargaining"). Moreover, Nussbaum links literacy to the ability for women to effectively communicate and collaborate with one another "to participate in a larger movement for political change."[[117]](#cite_note-121)

#### Challenges of increasing female literacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Challenges of increasing female literacy")]

Social barriers can limit opportunities to increase literacy skills among women and girls; making literacy classes available can be ineffective when it conflicts with the use of the valuable limited time of women and girls.[[118]](#cite_note-122) School-age girls may face more expectations than their male counterparts to perform household work and care for younger siblings.[[119]](#cite_note-World-Literacy-Foundation-2015-123) Generational dynamics can also perpetuate these disparities; illiterate parents may not readily appreciate the value of literacy for their daughters, particularly in traditional, rural societies with expectations that girls will remain at home.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

A [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and [International Center for Research on Women](/wiki/International_Center_for_Research_on_Women "International Center for Research on Women") review of academic literature concluded that [child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage"), which predominantly impacts girls, tends to reduce literacy levels.[[121]](#cite_note-125) A 2008 analysis of the issue in Bangladesh found that for every additional year a girl's marriage is delayed, her likelihood of literacy increases by 5.6%.[[122]](#cite_note-126) Similarly, a 2014 study found a correlation that in sub-Saharan Africa, marrying early significantly decreases a girl's probability of literacy, even after accounting for other variables.[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128)

#### Gender gap for boys in developed countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Gender gap for boys in developed countries")]

While women and girls comprise the majority of the global illiterate population, in many [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries"), a literacy-[gender gap](/wiki/Gender_gap "Gender gap") exists in the opposite direction. Data from the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") has consistently shown the literacy underachievement of boys within member countries of the OECD ([Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development")).[[125]](#cite_note-129) In view of such findings, many education specialists have recommended changing classroom practices to better accommodate boys' learning styles and removing any gender stereotypes that may create the perception that reading and writing are feminine activities.[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-131)

### Socioeconomic impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Socioeconomic impact")]

Many policy analysts consider literacy rates to be a crucial measure of the value of a region's [human capital](/wiki/Human_capital "Human capital"). For example, literate people can be more easily trained than illiterate people and generally have a higher socioeconomic status;[[128]](#cite_note-132) thus, they enjoy better health and employment prospects. The international community has come to consider literacy as a key facilitator and goal of development.[[129]](#cite_note-133) In regard to the [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") adopted by the UN in 2015, the [UNESCO Institute for Lifelong Learning](/wiki/UNESCO_Institute_for_Lifelong_Learning "UNESCO Institute for Lifelong Learning") has declared the "central role of literacy in responding to sustainable development challenges such as health, social equality, economic empowerment and environmental sustainability."[[130]](#cite_note-134)

A majority of prisoners have been found to be illiterate, and in Edinburgh prison, winner of the 2010 Libraries Change Lives Award, "the library has become the cornerstone of the prison's literacy strategy", reducing recidivism and reoffending and allowing incarcerated people to work toward attaining higher socioeconomic status once released.[[131]](#cite_note-135)

#### Effects on literacy learning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Effects on literacy learning")]

As socioeconomics affects brain development and brain functions are heavily involved in processing both input and output, a learner's environment can affect the cognitive process of learning [how to read](/wiki/Reading#Learning_to_read "Reading") and write.[[132]](#cite_note-Hackman-2015-136) Before a child enters a school setting, their executive function is influenced by their home environment.[[133]](#cite_note-Haft-2017-137) Research demonstrates that for children who grow up in poverty, their socioeconomic circumstances severely strain their "neuro-endocrine and brain function".[[133]](#cite_note-Haft-2017-137) This affects a child's ability to regulate environmental stimuli, process and structure information, and plan and effectively execute tasks that involve their working memory[[132]](#cite_note-Hackman-2015-136)—all of these are necessary cognitive facilities to successfully learn how to read and write. Living in poverty is stressful for all involved but is cognitively damaging for young children.[[134]](#cite_note-138)

A study done by [NICHD](/wiki/NICHD "NICHD") indicates that socioeconomics plays a role for children who are young when the family experiences poverty, but shows no indication of adverse effects on reading achievement or behavior for adolescents entering poverty.[[135]](#cite_note-139)
The data extensively shows that children from low socioeconomic backgrounds had poorer literacy performance, especially in reading. A study done by the OECD, which included over 25 countries in Europe, found that in all studied countries, students who lived in low-income households scored lower in reading than students who lived in high-income households.[[136]](#cite_note-140)

Parenting also affects a child's literacy. Field research was done by collecting data from families that were upper, middle, or lower class, or on welfare. The results found that, in a 100-hour week, children in upper-class households experienced an average of over 200,000 words, those in middle- and lower-class households heard about 125,000 words, and children from households on welfare were exposed to the fewest words—62,000 words. This indicates that a child from an upper-class family would be exposed to 8 million more words than a child from a family on welfare.[[137]](#cite_note-141) Outside of word exposure, which is essential for word acquisition, the National Center for Educational Statistics found that 41.9% of children from low-income families scored substantially lower on most reading achievements for grades 4, 8, and 12 in 2013.[[138]](#cite_note-142)

According to a study performed by [ANOVA](/wiki/ANOVA "ANOVA"), multiple socioeconomic variables influence children, such as parental education level, parental occupation, health history, and even usage of technology within the home. With these factors in mind, their study showed that young children are especially susceptible to environmental factors, meaning socioeconomics affects them cognitively and can have adverse effects as their brains continue to develop.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, another study done by the [National Longitudinal Survey of Youth](/wiki/National_Longitudinal_Survey_of_Youth "National Longitudinal Survey of Youth") (NLSY) around 2012 suggesteda slightly different conclusion.[[139]](#cite_note-143) While the study agrees that poverty negatively affects childhood literacy, some nuances are added. In both studies, children who experienced poverty scored lower in reading assessments, but the NLSY's study noted that the duration of poverty altered the literacy outcome.[[140]](#cite_note-Lee-2009-144) It found that children ages 5–11 who experienced "persistent poverty" were more adversely affected than their [peers](/wiki/Peer_group "Peer group") who never experienced poverty. The study acknowledged that other factors affected these children's reading scores, particularly maternal influence. The mothers of these households were scaled based on a "home environment" score, which measured their emotional and verbal responsiveness, acceptance, and involvement with the child and organization. Households experiencing poverty tended to have lower scores, and lower scores [correlated](/wiki/Correlate "Correlate") with lower reading levels. The study also showed that the effects of poverty on child literacy differed by ethnicity, culture, and gender.[[140]](#cite_note-Lee-2009-144)

### Health impacts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Health impacts")]

Print illiteracy generally corresponds with less knowledge about modern health, hygiene, and nutritional practices, and a lack of knowledge can exacerbate a range of health issues.[[141]](#cite_note-145) Within developing countries in particular, literacy rates also have implications for [child mortality](/wiki/Child_mortality "Child mortality"); in these contexts, children of literate mothers are 50% more likely to live past age 5 than children of illiterate mothers.[[119]](#cite_note-World-Literacy-Foundation-2015-123) Therefore, public health research has increasingly focused on the potential for literacy skills to allow women to more successfully access healthcare and thereby facilitate gains in child health.[[142]](#cite_note-146) Literacy significantly influences healthcare utilization and is a primary predictor for subnational health insurance enrollment disparities.[[143]](#cite_note-147)

A 2014 descriptive research survey project correlates literacy levels with the socioeconomic status of women in [Oyo State](/wiki/Oyo_State "Oyo State"), Nigeria. The study shows that developing literacy in the region will bring "economic empowerment and will encourage rural women to practice hygiene, which will in turn lead to the reduction of birth and death rates."[[144]](#cite_note-148)

### Economic impacts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economic impacts")]

Literacy can increase job opportunities and access to [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education"). In 2009, the National Adult Literacy Agency in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") commissioned a [cost–benefit analysis](/wiki/Cost%E2%80%93benefit_analysis "Cost–benefit analysis") of adult literacy training, which concluded that there were economic gains for the individuals, the companies they worked for, and the [Exchequer](/wiki/Exchequer "Exchequer"), as well as the economy and the country as a whole (e.g., increased [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP")).[[145]](#cite_note-149)

[Korotayev](/wiki/Andrey_Korotayev "Andrey Korotayev") and coauthors found a rather significant correlation between the level of literacy in the early 19th century and successful modernization and economic breakthroughs in the late 20th century, as "literate people could be characterized by a greater innovative-activity level, which provides opportunities for modernization, development, and economic growth."[[146]](#cite_note-150)

### Lifespan development and promotion efforts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Lifespan development and promotion efforts")]

While informal learning within the home can play an important role in literacy development, gains in childhood literacy often occur in primary school settings. Continuing the global expansion of public education is thus a frequent focus of literacy advocates.[[101]](#cite_note-Wagner-2016-105): 103–104 These kinds of broad improvements in education often require centralized efforts by national governments; however, local literacy projects implemented by NGOs can play an important role, particularly in rural contexts.[[147]](#cite_note-151)

Funding for both youth and adult literacy programs often comes from large international development organizations. [USAID](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development"), for example, steered donors like the [Gates Foundation](/wiki/Gates_Foundation "Gates Foundation") and the [Global Partnership for Education](/wiki/Global_Partnership_for_Education "Global Partnership for Education") toward the issue of childhood literacy by developing the Early Grade Reading Assessment.[[148]](#cite_note-152) Advocacy groups like the [National Institute of Adult Continuing Education](/wiki/NIACE "NIACE") have frequently called upon international organizations such as UNESCO, the [International Labour Organization](/wiki/International_Labour_Organization "International Labour Organization"), the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"), and the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") to prioritize support for adult women's literacy.[[149]](#cite_note-153) Efforts to increase adult literacy often encompass other development priorities as well; for example, initiatives in Ethiopia, Morocco, and India have combined adult literacy programs with vocational skills trainings in order to encourage program enrollment and address the complex needs of women (and other marginalized groups) who lack economic opportunities.[[150]](#cite_note-Kairies-2013-154)

In 2013, the [UNESCO Institute for Lifelong Learning](/wiki/UNESCO_Institute_for_Lifelong_Learning "UNESCO Institute for Lifelong Learning") published a set of case studies[[150]](#cite_note-Kairies-2013-154) on programs that successfully improved female literacy rates. The report features countries from a variety of regions and differing income levels, reflecting the general global consensus on "the need to empower women through the acquisition of literacy skills."[[150]](#cite_note-Kairies-2013-154): 7 Part of the impetus for UNESCO's focus on literacy is a broader effort to respond to globalization and "the shift towards knowledge-based societies" that it has produced.[[151]](#cite_note-155) While globalization presents emerging challenges, it also provides new opportunities. Many education and development specialists are hopeful that new [ICTs](/wiki/Information_and_communications_technology "Information and communications technology") will expand literacy learning opportunities for children and adults, even in countries that have historically struggled to improve literacy rates through more conventional means.[[101]](#cite_note-Wagner-2016-105): 112

Although most people acquire literacy during childhood, it continues to develop throughout life;[[152]](#cite_note-Lechner-2021-156) literacy is not a skill that is fixed once a person leaves school but remains malleable across the entire lifespan. Among adults, both gains and losses in literacy occur in roughly equal measure, sometimes over relatively short periods of a few years.[[152]](#cite_note-Lechner-2021-156) Even adults with very low literacy levels can acquire literacy over time.[[153]](#cite_note-157)[[154]](#cite_note-158) Whether a person experiences gains or losses depends on a range of factors, and one of the key factors are the demands and opportunities to engage in literary practices in the workplace, home, or other contexts.[[155]](#cite_note-159)[[156]](#cite_note-160)

### Literacy as a development indicator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Literacy as a development indicator")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Youth_and_adult_literacy_rate%2C_2000%E2%80%932016_and_projections_to_2030.svg/250px-Youth_and_adult_literacy_rate%2C_2000%E2%80%932016_and_projections_to_2030.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Youth_and_adult_literacy_rate,_2000%E2%80%932016_and_projections_to_2030.svg)

Youth and adult literacy rate, 2000–2016 and projections to 2030

The [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), produced by the [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") (UNDP), uses education as one of its three indicators. Originally, adult literacy represented two-thirds of this education index weight. In 2010, however, the UNDP replaced the adult literacy measure with mean years of schooling. A 2011 UNDP research paper frames this change as a way to "ensure current relevance", arguing that gains in global literacy already achieved between 1970 and 2010 mean that literacy will be "unlikely to be as informative of the future."[[157]](#cite_note-161) Other scholars, however, have since warned against overlooking the importance of literacy as an indicator and goal for development, particularly for marginalized groups such as women and rural populations.[[158]](#cite_note-162)

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), along with the [UNESCO Institute for Statistics](/wiki/UNESCO_Institute_for_Statistics "UNESCO Institute for Statistics"), has developed the Learning Poverty concept and an associated measure that measures the proportion of students who are unable to read and understand a simple story by age 10. In low- and middle-income countries, 53% of children are "learning-poor", as are up to 80% of children in poor countries.[[159]](#cite_note-Learning-Poverty-163) In fact, these new measures indicate that these high rates of illiteracy are an "early warning sign that [SDG 4](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goal_4 "Sustainable Development Goal 4") for education and all related global goals are in jeopardy."[[159]](#cite_note-Learning-Poverty-163) Current progress in improving literacy rates is seen as much too slow to meet the SDG goals, as at the current rate, approximately 43% of children will still be learning poorly by 2030.[[159]](#cite_note-Learning-Poverty-163)

The [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") (PISA) assesses children on reading and math skills at age 15. PISA-D encourages and facilitates PISA testing in low- and middle-income countries.[[160]](#cite_note-164) In 2019, "PISA-D results reveal exceptionally low scores for participating countries. Only 23 percent of students tested achieved the minimum level of proficiency in reading, compared with 80 percent of OECD."[[161]](#cite_note-PISAD-2019-165) Minimum proficiency requires students to "read 'simple and familiar texts and understand them literally', as well as demonstrating some ability to connect pieces of information and draw inferences."[[161]](#cite_note-PISAD-2019-165)

The [Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies](/wiki/Programme_for_the_International_Assessment_of_Adult_Competencies "Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies") (PIAAC) assesses literacy, [numeracy](/wiki/Numeracy "Numeracy") and [problem solving](/wiki/Problem_solving "Problem solving") for working age population ages 16 to 65.[[14]](#cite_note-PIAAC-14)

### Measuring literacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Measuring literacy")]

In 2020, the UNESCO Institute for Statistics estimated the global literacy rate at 86.68%.[[162]](#cite_note-166) It is important to understand how literacy rates have been measured in the past as well as how they are currently being measured. Starting in 1975, the head of a household answered a simple yes-or-no question asking whether household members could read and write; in 1988, some countries started using self-reporting as well.[[163]](#cite_note-How-is-literacy-measured-167)

Self-reported data is subjective and has several limitations. First, a simple yes-or-no question does not capture the continuum of literacy. Second, self-reports are dependent on what each individual interprets "reading" and "writing" to mean. In some cultures, drawing a picture may be understood as writing one's name. Lastly, many of the surveys asked one individual to report literacy on behalf of others, which "introduces further noise, in particular when it comes to estimating literacy among women and children, since these groups are less often considered 'head of household'".[[163]](#cite_note-How-is-literacy-measured-167)

In 2007, several countries began introducing literacy tests as a more accurate measurement of literacy rates, including Liberia, South Korea, Guyana, Kenya, and Bangladesh.[[163]](#cite_note-How-is-literacy-measured-167) However, in 2016, the majority of counties still reported literacy through either self-reported measures or other indirect estimates.[[163]](#cite_note-How-is-literacy-measured-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Students-in-grade-2-who-cant-read-a-single-word-ca-2015.png/250px-Students-in-grade-2-who-cant-read-a-single-word-ca-2015.png)](/wiki/File:Students-in-grade-2-who-cant-read-a-single-word-ca-2015.png)

Students in grade 2 who can't read a single word

These indirect measurements are potentially problematic, as many countries measure literacy based on years of schooling. In Greece, an individual is considered literate if they have finished six years of primary education, while in Paraguay, individuals are considered literate if they have completed just two years of primary school.[[163]](#cite_note-How-is-literacy-measured-167)

However, emerging research reveals that educational attainment (e.g., years of schooling) does not perfectly correlate with literacy. Literacy tests show that in many low-income countries, a large proportion of students who have attended two years of primary school cannot read a single word. These rates are as high as 90% of second-grade students in Malawi, 85.4% in rural India, 83% in Ghana, and 64% in Uganda.[[164]](#cite_note-168) In India, over 50% of Grade 5 students have not mastered Grade 2 literacy. In Nigeria, only about 1 in 10 women who completed Grade 6 can read a single sentence in their native language.[[165]](#cite_note-169) This data reveals that literacy rates measured by using years of schooling as a proxy are potentially unreliable and do not reflect the true literacy rates of populations.

### Literacy as a human right

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Literacy as a human right")]

Unlike medieval times, when reading and writing skills were restricted to a few elites and the clergy, literacy skills are now expected from every member of society.[[166]](#cite_note-170) Literacy is therefore considered a human right, essential for [lifelong learning](/wiki/Lifelong_learning "Lifelong learning") and social change, as supported by the 1996 Report of the International Commission on Education for the Twenty-First Century and the 1997 Hamburg Declaration:

> Literacy, broadly conceived as the basic knowledge and skills needed by all in a rapidly changing world, is a fundamental human right. (...) There are millions, the majority of whom are women, who lack opportunities to learn or who have insufficient skills to be able to assert this right. The challenge is to enable them to do so. This will often imply the creation of preconditions for learning through awareness raising and empowerment. Literacy is also a catalyst for participation in social, cultural, political and economic activities, and for learning throughout life.[[167]](#cite_note-171)[[168]](#cite_note-172)[[169]](#cite_note-173)

In 2016, the European Literacy Policy Network (an association of European literacy professionals) published a document entitled the *European Declaration of the Right to Literacy*.[[170]](#cite_note-174) It states that:

> Everyone in Europe has the right to acquire literacy. EU Member States should ensure that people of all ages, regardless of social class, religion, ethnicity, origin and gender, are provided with the necessary resources and opportunities to develop sufficient and sustainable literacy skills in order to effectively understand and use written communication be in handwritten, in print or digital form.[[171]](#cite_note-175)

## Teaching literacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Teaching literacy")]

Main articles: [Reading § Teaching reading](/wiki/Reading#Teaching_reading "Reading"), and [Composition studies](/wiki/Composition_studies "Composition studies")

|  |  |
