[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Brain_pathways_for_mirror_discrimination_learning_during_literacy_acquisition.jpg/500px-Brain_pathways_for_mirror_discrimination_learning_during_literacy_acquisition.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brain_pathways_for_mirror_discrimination_learning_during_literacy_acquisition.jpg)

Brain areas involved in literacy acquisition

In school, reading and writing are often taught as separate skills. However, children show curiosity about the written word and begin to experiment with both in a process of [emergent literacy](/wiki/Emergent_literacy "Emergent literacy") and making sense of (and using) the [writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") they see used around them. Every new piece of writing draws on previous reading through a process of [intertextuality](/wiki/Intertextuality "Intertextuality"), sometimes explicitly through [citation](/wiki/Citation "Citation"), as in [academic writing](/wiki/Academic_writing "Academic writing"), and writing about reading is one of the major approaches for teaching writing in higher education.[[172]](#cite_note-176) Intertextuality, however, can also be implicit through well-known, recognizable phrases from specific works or [genres](/wiki/Genre "Genre") or through the development of a distinct [writing style](/wiki/Writing_style "Writing style"). Evidence has supported the integration of reading and writing at all levels of schooling, as improvement in one area supports the other.[[173]](#cite_note-177)[[174]](#cite_note-178) A series of metastudies have examined the effectiveness of various methods of teaching writing, revealing that attention to context, cognitive/motivational factors, and the instruction strategy, among other things, are important.[[175]](#cite_note-179)[[176]](#cite_note-180)

Critiques of autonomous models of literacy notwithstanding, the belief that [reading](/wiki/Reading_(process) "Reading (process)") development is key to literacy remains dominant, at least in the United States, where it is understood as the progression of skills that begins with the ability to understand spoken words and decode written words and culminates in the deep understanding of the text. Reading development involves a range of complex language underpinnings, including awareness of speech sounds ([phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology")), spelling patterns ([orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography")), word meaning ([semantics](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics")), [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax"), and patterns of word formation ([morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)")), all of which provide a necessary platform for reading fluency and comprehension. Once these skills are acquired, it is believed a reader can attain full language literacy, which includes the abilities to apply to printed material [critical analysis](/wiki/Critical_thinking "Critical thinking"), inference, and synthesis; to write with accuracy and coherence; and to use information and insights from text as the basis for informed decisions and creative thought.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For this reason, teaching English reading literacy in the United States is dominated by a focus on a set of discrete decoding skills. From this perspective, literacy—or rather, reading—comprises a number of sub-skills that can be taught to students. These sub-skills include [phonological awareness](/wiki/Phonological_awareness "Phonological awareness"), [phonics](/wiki/Phonics "Phonics") decoding, fluency, [comprehension](/wiki/Reading_comprehension "Reading comprehension"), and [vocabulary](/wiki/Vocabulary "Vocabulary"). Mastering each of these sub-skills is necessary for students to become proficient readers.[[177]](#cite_note-181)

From this same perspective, readers of [alphabetic languages](/wiki/Alphabetic_language "Alphabetic language") must understand the [alphabetic principle](/wiki/Alphabetic_principle "Alphabetic principle") to master basic reading skills. For this purpose, a writing system is "alphabetic" if it uses symbols to represent [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") (individual language sounds),[[178]](#cite_note-Sedlorg-2008-182) though the degree of correspondence between letters and sounds varies between alphabetic languages. [Syllabic writing systems](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary") (such as [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system") [kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")) use a symbol to represent a single syllable, and [logographic writing systems](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") (such as [Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese")) use a symbol to represent a [morpheme](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme").[[179]](#cite_note-183)

There are a number of approaches to [teaching reading](/wiki/Reading#Teaching_reading "Reading").[[180]](#cite_note-184) Each is shaped by its assumptions about what literacy is and how it is best learned by students. [Phonics](/wiki/Phonics "Phonics") instruction, for example, focuses on reading at the level of letters or symbols and their sounds (i.e., [sublexical](/wiki/Dual-route_hypothesis_to_reading_aloud#Nonlexical_or_Sublexical "Dual-route hypothesis to reading aloud")).[[181]](#cite_note-185) It teaches readers to decode the letters, or groups of letters, that make up a word. A common method of teaching phonics is [synthetic phonics](/wiki/Synthetic_phonics "Synthetic phonics"), in which a novice reader pronounces each individual sound and blends them to pronounce the whole word. Another approach is embedded phonics instruction, used more often in [whole language](/wiki/Whole_language "Whole language") reading instruction, in which novice readers learn about the individual letters in words on a just-in-time, just-in-place basis that is tailored to meet each student's reading and writing learning needs.[[178]](#cite_note-Sedlorg-2008-182) That is, teachers provide phonics instruction opportunistically, within the context of stories or student writing that feature repeat instances of a particular letter or group of letters. Embedded instruction combines letter-sound knowledge with the use of meaningful context to read new and difficult words.[[182]](#cite_note-186) Techniques such as [directed listening and thinking activities](/wiki/Directed_listening_and_thinking_activity "Directed listening and thinking activity") can be used to aid children in learning how to read and in [reading comprehension](/wiki/Reading_comprehension "Reading comprehension"). For students at both primary and secondary levels, writing about what they read as they are learning to write has been found to also be effective in improving their reading skills.[[183]](#cite_note-187)

The two most commonly used approaches to reading instruction are [structured literacy](/wiki/Structured_literacy "Structured literacy") instruction and [balanced literacy](/wiki/Balanced_literacy "Balanced literacy") instruction. The structured literacy approach explicitly and systematically focuses on phonological awareness, word recognition, phonics, decoding, spelling, and syntax at both the sentence and paragraph levels.[[184]](#cite_note-An-Explanation-of-Structured-Literacy-188) The balanced literacy approach, as the name suggests, balances emphasis on phonics and decoding; shared, guided, and independent reading; and [grapheme](/wiki/Grapheme "Grapheme") representations with context and imagery.[[184]](#cite_note-An-Explanation-of-Structured-Literacy-188) Both approaches have their critics—those who oppose structured literacy claim that by restricting students to phonemes, their fluency development is limited; critics of balanced literacy claim that if phonics and decoding instruction are neglected, students will have to rely on compensatory strategies when confronted with unfamiliar text.[[184]](#cite_note-An-Explanation-of-Structured-Literacy-188)[[e]](#cite_note-190)

These strategies are taught to students as part of the balanced literacy approach based on a theory about reading development called the [three-cueing system](/wiki/Reading#Three_cueing_system_(Searchlights_model) "Reading"). As the name suggests, the three-cueing system uses three cues to determine the meaning of words: grapho-phonetic cues (letter-sound relationships); syntactic cues (grammatical structure); and semantic cues (a word making sense in context).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, cognitive neuroscientist Mark Seidenberg and professor Timothy Shanahan do not support the theory. They say the three-cueing system's value in reading instruction "is a magnificent work of the imagination", and it developed not because teachers lack integrity, commitment, motivation, sincerity, or intelligence, but because they "were poorly trained and advised" about [the science of reading](/wiki/Reading#Science_of_reading "Reading"). In England, the simple view of reading and synthetic phonics are intended to replace "the searchlights multi-cueing model".[[186]](#cite_note-191)[[187]](#cite_note-192)[[188]](#cite_note-193)[*[specify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In his 2009 book *Reading in the Brain*, cognitive neuroscientist [Stanislas Dehaene](/wiki/Stanislas_Dehaene "Stanislas Dehaene") said "cognitive psychology directly refutes any notion of teaching via a 'global' or 'whole language' method." He goes on to talk about "the myth of whole-word reading", saying it has been refuted by recent[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] experiments. "We do not recognize a printed word through a holistic grasping of its contours, because our brain breaks it down into letters and graphemes."[[189]](#cite_note-194)

However, a 2012 hypothesis proposed that reading might be acquired naturally, in the same manner as spoken language, if print is constantly available at an early age.[[190]](#cite_note-195) According to this theory, if an appropriate form of written text is made available before formal schooling begins, reading should be learned inductively, emerge naturally, and have no significant negative consequences. This proposal challenges the commonly held belief that written language requires formal instruction and schooling; thus, its success would change current views of literacy and schooling. Using developments in behavioral science and technology, Technology-Assisted Reading Acquisition (TARA), an interactive system, would enable young pre-literate children to accurately perceive and learn the properties of written language through simple exposure to the written form.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In Australia, a number of state governments have introduced Reading Challenges to improve literacy. The Premier's Reading Challenge in South Australia, launched by [Premier Mike Rann](/wiki/Mike_Rann "Mike Rann"), has one of the highest participation rates in the world for reading challenges. It has been embraced by more than 95% of public, private, and religious schools.[[191]](#cite_note-196)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Post-conflict settings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Post-conflict settings")]

Programs have been implemented in regions that have an ongoing conflict or are in a post-conflict stage. The Norwegian Refugee Council Pack program has been used in 13 post-conflict countries since 2003. The program organizers believe that daily routines and otherwise predictable activities help ease the transition from war to peace. Learners can select one area of vocational training for a year-long period; they also complete required courses in agriculture, life skills, literacy, and numeracy. Results have shown that active participation and management of the members of the program are important to the success of the program. These programs share the use of integrated basic education, e.g., literacy, numeracy, scientific knowledge, local history and culture, native and mainstream language skills, and apprenticeships.[[192]](#cite_note-Bernhardt-2014-197)

### Teaching migrant, immigrant, and non-native users

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Teaching migrant, immigrant, and non-native users")]

Although there is considerable awareness that language deficiencies, including a lack of proficiency, are disadvantageous to immigrants settling into a new country, there is a lack of [pedagogical](/wiki/Pedagogical "Pedagogical") approaches to teaching literacy to migrant English-language learners (ELLs). Harvard scholar Catherine Snow called for the gap to be addressed: "The TESOL field needs a concerted research effort to inform literacy instruction for such children—to determine when to start literacy instruction and how to adapt it to the LS reader's needs."[[193]](#cite_note-198) Recent developments to address the gap in teaching literacy to foreign language learners[[f]](#cite_note-199) have been ongoing, with promising results seen with a curricular framework from the Harvard Graduate School of Education, which integrates Teaching for Understanding.[[194]](#cite_note-200)

A series of pilot projects have been carried out in the Middle East and Africa,[[195]](#cite_note-Patl-2016-201) and significant interest from the learners has been seen in the use of visual arts as springboards for literacy-oriented instruction. In one project, migrant women were provided with cameras and took the instructor on a walking tour of their village. There, they photographed places and activities that would later be used for writings about their daily lives—in essence, a narrative of life. Other primers for writing activities include painting, sketching, and other craft projects.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Sample_sketch_of_milestone.jpg/250px-Sample_sketch_of_milestone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sample_sketch_of_milestone.jpg)

Sample milestone sketch

In another series of pilot studies, alternatives to instructing literacy to migrant [English-language learners](/wiki/English-language_learner "English-language learner") were investigated,[[196]](#cite_note-Pellerine-202) starting with simple trials aiming to test the effects of teaching photography to participants with no prior photography background and then painting and sketching activities that could later be integrated into a larger pedagogical initiative. In efforts to develop alternative approaches for literacy instruction utilizing visual arts, work was carried out with Afghan laborers, Bangladeshi tailors, Emirati media students, internal Ethiopian migrants (both laborers and university students), and a street child.[[195]](#cite_note-Patl-2016-201)[[196]](#cite_note-Pellerine-202)[[197]](#cite_note-Perez-203)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Migrant_Woman_Reviewing_Photography%2C_Making_Seletions_for_Writing.jpeg/250px-Migrant_Woman_Reviewing_Photography%2C_Making_Seletions_for_Writing.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Migrant_Woman_Reviewing_Photography,_Making_Seletions_for_Writing.jpeg)

Reviewing photos after a photowalk

It should be pointed out that in these challenging contexts, sometimes the teaching of literacy may have unforeseen barriers. The *EL Gazette* reported that in the trials carried out in Ethiopia, for example, it was found that all ten of the participants had problems with vision.[[197]](#cite_note-Perez-203) In order to overcome this or avoid such challenges, preliminary health checks can help inform pre-teaching in order to better assist in the teaching and learning of literacy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Using a visual arts approach to literacy instruction can provide benefits by incorporating a traditional literacy approach (reading and writing) while also addressing 21st-century digital literacy through the use of digital cameras and posting images onto the web. Many scholars, such as Hutchison and Woodward, feel that it is necessary to include digital literacy under the traditional umbrella of literacy instruction, specifically when engaging second language learners.[[198]](#cite_note-204)

A visual arts approach to literary instruction for migrant populations can also be blended with core curricular goals.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Integrating_Content_for_Language_Instruction_to_MELLs.jpg/250px-Integrating_Content_for_Language_Instruction_to_MELLs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Integrating_Content_for_Language_Instruction_to_MELLs.jpg)

Integrating Common Core content into language training with MELL

A pressing challenge in education is the instruction of literacy to migrant English-language learners (MELLs), a term coined by Pellerine and not limited to English. "Due to the growing share of immigrants in many Western societies, there has been increasing concern for the degree to which immigrants acquire language that is spoken in the destination country".[[199]](#cite_note-205)

While learning literacy in one's first language can be challenging, the challenge becomes even more cognitively demanding when learning a second language. The task can become considerably more difficult when confronted by a migrant who has made a sudden change by immigrating and requires the second language immediately upon arrival. In most instances, a migrant will not have the opportunity to start school again in grade one and acquire the language naturally; instead, alternative interventions need to take place. In these cases, a visual arts approach can be helpful—taking a photo, sketching an event, or painting an image have been seen as effective ways to understand the intention of the learner as they can incorporate [orality](/wiki/Orality "Orality").[[200]](#cite_note-206)[[201]](#cite_note-207)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Including_Orality.jpg/500px-Including_Orality.jpg)](/wiki/File:Including_Orality.jpg)

Including orality

In the above image, from left to right:[[202]](#cite_note-208)

* An image taken during a phototour of the participant's village. This image is of the individual at her shop with one of the products she sells: dung for cooking fuel. The image helps the instructor understand the realities of the participant's daily life, and most importantly, it gives the participant the opportunity to determine what is important to them.
* An image of a student explaining to a group and elaborating on a drawn series of milestones in her life. This student had a very basic ability and, with some help, was able to write brief captions under the images. While she speaks, her story is recorded to help her understand and develop it in the new language.
* A painting created by composite in a graphics editing program. With further training, participants can learn how to blend images, thereby introducing elements of digital literacy that are beneficial in many spheres of life in the 21st century.

In a study based in Ethiopia, participants were asked to rate their preference for activity on a scale of 1–10. The survey prompt was: "On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate photography as an activity that helped you get inspiration for your writing activities (think of enjoyment and usefulness)?" The activities used as primers for writing were rated, in order of preference:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Photography: 97%
* Oral presentations/sharing your art: 92%
* Process painting: 84%
* Painting: 82%
* Sketching: 78%
* Gluing activities: 72%
* Stencil/tracing activities: 60%

More research would need to be conducted to confirm such trends.

Authorship programs have been successful in bringing student work together in book format as part of the program's culmination. These books can be used to document learning, and more importantly, to reinforce language and content goals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/Covers_for_Authorship_Initiatives_to_Promote_Literacy_via_Visual_Art_with_ELLs.jpg/250px-Covers_for_Authorship_Initiatives_to_Promote_Literacy_via_Visual_Art_with_ELLs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Covers_for_Authorship_Initiatives_to_Promote_Literacy_via_Visual_Art_with_ELLs.jpg)

Sample covers of completed authorship-created books

The collection of such writings into books can trigger both intrinsic and extrinsic motivation. Feedback by students involved in such initiatives indicates that the healthy pressures of collective and collaborative work were beneficial.[[203]](#cite_note-209)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

On average, girls do better than boys at English, yet nearly one in ten young adult women have poor reading and writing skills in the UK in the 21st century, which seriously damages their employment prospects. Many are trapped in poverty but hide their lack of reading skills due to social stigma.[[204]](#cite_note-210)

##### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: England")]

Literacy is first documented to have occurred in the area of modern England on 24 September 54 BCE, when [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") and [Quintus Cicero](/wiki/Quintus_Cicero "Quintus Cicero") wrote to [Marcus Cicero](/wiki/Marcus_Cicero "Marcus Cicero") "from the nearest shores of Britain".[[205]](#cite_note-211) Literacy was widespread under Roman rule but became very rare, limited almost entirely to churchmen, after the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"). In 12th and 13th century England, the ability to recite a particular passage from the Bible ([Psalm 51](/wiki/Psalm_51 "Psalm 51")) in Latin entitled a [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") defendant to the [benefit of clergy](/wiki/Benefit_of_clergy "Benefit of clergy") and trial before an [ecclesiastical court](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_court "Ecclesiastical court"), where sentences were more lenient, instead of a secular one, where hanging was a likely sentence. Thus, literate defendants often claimed the benefit of clergy, while an illiterate person who had memorized the psalm used in the literacy test could also claim the benefit of clergy.[[206]](#cite_note-212)

Despite lacking a system of free and compulsory primary schooling, England reached near universal literacy in the 19th century as a result of shared, informal learning provided by family members, fellow workers, or benevolent employers. Even with near-universal literacy, the gap between male and female rates persisted until the early 20th century. Many women in the West during the 19th century were able to read but unable to write.[[207]](#cite_note-213)

##### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Wales")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Literacy "Special:EditPage/Literacy") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Formal higher education in the arts and sciences in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), from the Middle Ages to the 18th century, was limited to the wealthy and the clergy. Following the Roman occupation and the conquest by the English, education in Wales was at a low point during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"); in particular, formal education was only available in English while the majority of the population spoke only [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"). The first modern grammar schools were established in Welsh towns such as [Ruthin](/wiki/Ruthin "Ruthin"), [Brecon](/wiki/Brecon "Brecon"), and [Cowbridge](/wiki/Cowbridge "Cowbridge"). One of the first modern national education methods to use the native Welsh language was started by [Griffith Jones](/wiki/Griffith_Jones_(Llanddowror) "Griffith Jones (Llanddowror)") in 1731. Jones became rector of [Llanddowror](/wiki/Llanddowror "Llanddowror") in 1716 and remained there for the rest of his life. He organized and introduced a Welsh language-circulating school system, which was attractive and effective for Welsh speakers, while also teaching them English, which gave them access to broader educational sources. The circulating schools may have taught half the country's population to read. Literacy rates in Wales by the mid-18th century were one of the highest.

#### Continental Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Continental Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Adriaen_van_Ostade_007.jpg/250px-Adriaen_van_Ostade_007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adriaen_van_Ostade_007.jpg)

Dutch schoolmaster and children, 1662

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Russa_literacy_1897.jpg/250px-Russa_literacy_1897.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russa_literacy_1897.jpg)

Until the beginning of 20th century, most of the population in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") was illiterate (map of [1897 census](/wiki/Russian_Empire_census "Russian Empire census") literacy data).

The ability to read did not necessarily mean the ability to write. The [1686 church law](/wiki/Swedish_Church_Law_1686 "Swedish Church Law 1686") (*kyrkolagen*) of the Kingdom of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") (modern Sweden, [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), and [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia")) made literacy compulsory, and by 1800, the percent of people able to read was close to 100%.[[208]](#cite_note-214) This was directly dependent on the need to read religious texts in the [Lutheran faith](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"); as a result, literacy in these countries was specifically focused on reading.[[209]](#cite_note-Lions-2011-215) However, as late as the 19th century, many Swedes, especially women, could not write. [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") was an exception, as it achieved widespread literacy without formal schooling, libraries, or printed books via informal tuition by religious leaders and peasant teachers.[[209]](#cite_note-Lions-2011-215)

Historian [Ernest Gellner](/wiki/Ernest_Gellner "Ernest Gellner") argues that [Continental European](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") countries were far more successful in implementing educational reform because their governments were more willing to invest in the population as a whole.[[210]](#cite_note-216) Government oversight allowed countries to standardize curriculum and secure funding through legislation, thus enabling educational programs to have a broader reach.[[211]](#cite_note-217)

Although present-day concepts of literacy have much to do with the 15th-century invention of the [movable type](/wiki/Movable_type "Movable type") [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press"), it was not until the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") of the mid-19th century that paper and books became affordable to all classes of industrialized society. Until then, only a small percent of the population was literate, as only wealthy individuals and institutions could afford the materials. Even today[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit), the cost of paper and books is a barrier to universal literacy in some developing nations.[[212]](#cite_note-218)

On the other hand, historian [Harvey Graff](/wiki/Harvey_Graff "Harvey Graff") argues that the introduction of [compulsory education](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education") was, in part, an effort to control the type of literacy the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") had access to. According to Graff, learning was increasing outside of formal settings (e.g., schools), and this uncontrolled reading could lead to increased radicalization of the populace. In his view, mass schooling was meant to temper and control literacy, not spread it.[[213]](#cite_note-219) Graff also says, using the example of Sweden, that mass literacy can be achieved without formal schooling or instruction in writing.

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Mexico")]

In the last 40 years, the rate of illiteracy in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") has been steadily decreasing. In the 1960s, because the majority of the residents of the federal capital were illiterate, the planners of the [Mexico City Metro](/wiki/Mexico_City_Metro "Mexico City Metro") designed a series of unique icons to identify each station in the system in addition to its formal name. The [INEGI](/wiki/INEGI "INEGI")'s census data in 1970 showed a national average illiteracy rate of 25.8%, which had decreased to under 7% by the 2010 census. Mexico still has a gender educational bias—the illiteracy rate for women was 8.1% compared with 5.6% for men.[[214]](#cite_note-Analfabetismo-220)

Rates differ across regions and states. The states with the highest poverty rate had greater than 15% illiteracy in 2010: 17.8% in [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas"), 16.7% in [Guerrero](/wiki/Guerrero "Guerrero"), and 16.3% in [Oaxaca](/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca"). In contrast, the illiteracy rates in the Federal District (now part of [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City")) and in some northern states like [Nuevo León](/wiki/Nuevo_Le%C3%B3n "Nuevo León"), [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California"), and [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila") were below 3% in the 2010 census (2.1%, 2.2%, 2.6%, and 2.6%, respectively).[[214]](#cite_note-Analfabetismo-220)

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Brazil")]

In 1964, [Paulo Freire](/wiki/Paulo_Freire "Paulo Freire") was arrested and exiled for teaching peasants to read.[[215]](#cite_note-221) However, since democracy returned to Brazil, there has been a steady increase in the percentage of literate people.[[216]](#cite_note-222) Educators with the Axé project in the city of [Salvador, Bahía](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia"), attempt to improve literacy rates among [urban youth](/wiki/Youth_in_Brazil "Youth in Brazil"), especially youth living on the streets, through the use of cultural music and dances. Then, "they are encouraged to go on learning and become professional artists."[[192]](#cite_note-Bernhardt-2014-197): 284

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Africa")]

The literacy rates in Africa vary significantly between countries. The registered literacy rate in Libya was 86.1% in 2004,[[217]](#cite_note-223) and UNESCO says that the literacy rate in the region of [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea") is approximately 95%,[[218]](#cite_note-224)[[219]](#cite_note-225) while the literacy rate in [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") is approximately 27%.[[220]](#cite_note-226)

In sub-Saharan Africa, youth from wealthier families often have more educational opportunities to become literate than poorer youth, who may need to leave school because they are needed at home to farm or care for siblings.[[192]](#cite_note-Bernhardt-2014-197) Additionally, the rate of literacy has not improved enough to compensate for the effects of demographic growth. As a result, the number of illiterate adults has risen by 27% over the last 20 years, reaching 169 million in 2010.[[221]](#cite_note-227) Thus, out of the 775 million illiterate adults in the world in 2010, more than one fifth (20%) were in sub-Saharan Africa. The countries with the lowest levels of literacy in the world are also concentrated in this region, where adult literacy rates can be well below 50%.[[222]](#cite_note-Digital-Services-for-Education-in-Africa-2015-228)

#### Algeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Algeria")]

The literacy rate in [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") is 81.4%, attributable to the fact that education is compulsory and free up to age 17.[[232]](#cite_note-238)

#### Burkina Faso

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Burkina Faso")]

[Burkina Faso](/wiki/Burkina_Faso "Burkina Faso") has a very low literacy rate of 28.7%, defined as anyone at least 15 years of age who can read and write.[[224]](#cite_note-National-Population-and-Housing-Census-2014-230) To improve the literacy rate, the government has received at least 80 volunteer teachers. A severe lack of primary school teachers causes problems for any attempt to improve the literacy rate and school enrollment.[[233]](#cite_note-239)

#### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Egypt")]

[Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") has a relatively high literacy rate. The adult literacy rate in 2010 was estimated at 72%.[[226]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2013-232)

#### Ethiopia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Ethiopia")]

The Ethiopians are among the first literate people in sub-saharan Africa, having written, read, and created manuscripts in the ancient [Ge'ez language](/wiki/Ge%27ez_language "Ge'ez language") (an [Amharic language](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language")) since the 2nd century CE.[[228]](#cite_note-Weninger-2011-234) All boys learned to read the [Psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms") around the age of 7. The national literacy campaign introduced in 1978 increased literacy rates to between 37% (unofficial) and 63% (official) by 1984.[[234]](#cite_note-240)

#### Guinea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Guinea")]

[Guinea](/wiki/Guinea "Guinea") has a literacy rate of 41%, defined as anyone at least 15 years old who can read or write.[[224]](#cite_note-National-Population-and-Housing-Census-2014-230) Guinea was the first to use the Literacy, Conflict Resolution, and Peacebuilding (LCRP) project. This project was developed to increase agriculture production, develop key skills, resolve conflict, and improve literacy and numeracy skills. The LCRP worked within refugee camps near the border of Sierra Leone; however, this project only lasted from 1999 to 2001. There are several other international projects working within the country that have similar goals.[[235]](#cite_note-McCaffery-2005-241)

#### Kenya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Kenya")]

The literacy rate in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") among people below 20 years of age is over 70%, as the first 8 years of primary school are provided tuition-free by the government. In January 2008, the government began offering a limited program of free secondary education. Literacy is much higher among the young than among the older population, with the total being about 81.54% for the country. Most of this literacy, however, is at an elementary level—not secondary or advanced.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Mali

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Mali")]

Main article: [Education in Mali](/wiki/Education_in_Mali "Education in Mali")

In [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") in 2015, the adult literacy rate was 33%, one of the lowest in the world, with males having a 43.1% literacy rate and females having a 24.6% rate.[[236]](#cite_note-242) The government defines literacy as anyone at least 15 who can read or write.[[224]](#cite_note-National-Population-and-Housing-Census-2014-230) In recent years, the government of Mali and international organizations have taken steps to improve the literacy rate. The government recognized the slow progress and began creating ministries for basic education and literacy in their national languages in 2007; they also increased the education budget by 3%, when it was at 35% in 2007. The lack of literate adults causes the programs to be slowed—they need qualified female instructors, which is problematic as many men refuse to send female family members to be trained by male teachers.[[237]](#cite_note-243)

#### Mauritius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Mauritius")]

The adult [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") in [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") was estimated at 89.8% in 2011.[[229]](#cite_note-POPULATION-CENSUS-MAIN-RESULTS-2011-235) Male literacy was 92.3%, and female literacy was 87.3%.[[229]](#cite_note-POPULATION-CENSUS-MAIN-RESULTS-2011-235)

#### Niger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Niger")]

[Niger](/wiki/Niger "Niger") has an extremely low literacy rate of 28.7%, in part due to the gender gap—men have a literacy rate of 42.9%, while for women it is only 15.1%. The [Nigerien government](/wiki/Nigerien_government "Nigerien government") defines literacy as anyone who can read or write over the age of 15.[[224]](#cite_note-National-Population-and-Housing-Census-2014-230) The Niass [Tijāniyyah](/wiki/Tij%C4%81niyyah "Tijāniyyah"), a [Sufi](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") order, has started anti-poverty, empowerment, and literacy campaigns. The women in [Kiota](/wiki/Kiota "Kiota") had not attempted to improve their education or economic standing until Saida Oumul Khadiri Niass, known as Maman and married to a leader of the Niass Tijaniyya, talked to men and women throughout the community, changing the community's beliefs on appropriate behavior for women. Maman's efforts have allowed women in Kiota to own small businesses, sell in the market, attend literacy classes, and organize small associations that can give microloans. Maman personally teaches children in and around Kiota, with special attention to girls. Maman has her students require instructor permission to allow the girls' parents to marry their daughters early, increasing the amount of education these girls receive as well as delaying marriage, pregnancy, and having children.[[238]](#cite_note-244)

#### Senegal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Senegal")]

[Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal") has a literacy rate of 49.7%, defined as anyone who is at least 15 and can read and write.[[224]](#cite_note-National-Population-and-Housing-Census-2014-230) However, many students do not attend school long enough to be considered literate. The government did not begin actively attempting to improve the literacy rate until 1971, when it gave the responsibility to the Department for Vocational Training at the Secretariat for Youth and Sports. This department, and those that followed, had no clear policy on literacy until the Department of Literacy and Basic Education was formed in 1986. The government of Senegal relies heavily on funding from the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") to fund its school system.[[239]](#cite_note-245)

#### Somalia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Somalia")]

There is no reliable data on the nationwide literacy rate in [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"). A 2013 FSNAU survey indicates considerable differences per region, with the autonomous northeastern [Puntland](/wiki/Puntland "Puntland") region having the highest registered literacy rate at 72%.[[230]](#cite_note-FSNAU-236)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

#### Sierra Leone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Sierra Leone")]

The [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone") government defines literacy as anyone over the age of 15 who can read and write in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Mende](/wiki/Mende_language "Mende language"), [Temne](/wiki/Temne_language "Temne language"), or [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"). Official statistics put the literacy rate at 43.3%.[[231]](#cite_note-World-Factbook-237) Sierra Leone was the second country to use the Literacy, Conflict Resolution and Peacebuilding project. However, fighting near the city where the project was centered caused a delay until an arms amnesty was in place.[[235]](#cite_note-McCaffery-2005-241)

#### Afghanistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Afghanistan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Schoolgirls_in_Bamozai.JPG/250px-Schoolgirls_in_Bamozai.JPG)](/wiki/File:Schoolgirls_in_Bamozai.JPG)

Young school girls in [Paktia Province](/wiki/Paktia_Province "Paktia Province") of Afghanistan

According to [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") has one of the lowest literacy rates in the world. As of 2020, over 10 million youth and adults are illiterate. However, since 2016, the country has made significant progress. While in 2016–2017 the literacy rate was 34.8%, the UNESCO Institute for Statistics recently confirmed that it has increased to 43%. "That is a remarkable 8 percent increase." In addition, the literacy rate for youths aged 15–24 has substantially increased and now stands at 65%.[[240]](#cite_note-Literacy-rate-in-Afghanistan-246)

However, there are still a large number of people who lack literacy and opportunities to access continuing education. There is also a substantial gender gap: the literacy rate for men stands at 55%, while for women it is only 29.8%. The UNESCO Institute for Lifelong Learning has provided technical support to the government of Afghanistan since 2012, with the aim of improving the literacy skills of an estimated 1.2 million people.[[251]](#cite_note-257)

To improve the literacy rate, the US military taught Afghan Army recruits how to read before teaching them how to fire a weapon. In 2009, US commanders estimated that as many as 65% of recruits may be illiterate.[[252]](#cite_note-258)

#### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: China")]

Main article: [Education in the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Education_in_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Education in the People's Republic of China")

While literacy in Chinese can be assessed by reading comprehension tests, just as in other languages, historically, literacy has often been judged by the number of Chinese characters introduced during the speaker's schooling, with a few thousand considered the minimum for practical literacy.[[253]](#cite_note-259)

The CIA World Factbook says 96.7% of Chinese people are literate.[[242]](#cite_note-World-Factbook-China-248) In classical Chinese civilization, access to literacy for all classes originated with [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), where previously literacy was generally limited to the aristocracy, merchants, and priests.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: India")]

Main article: [Literacy in India](/wiki/Literacy_in_India "Literacy in India")

Literacy is defined by the [Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India](/wiki/Registrar_General_and_Census_Commissioner_of_India "Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India") as the ability of "a person aged 7 years and above to both write and read with understanding in any language." According to the [2011 census](/wiki/Census_of_India,_2011 "Census of India, 2011"), the literacy rate stood at 74%.[[254]](#cite_note-260)

#### Iran

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Iran")]

In 2023, the Iranian government stopped a literacy campaign that had begun in 1930, despite 9 million people still being reported as illiterate.[[255]](#cite_note-261) The government reported that elementary school education cost 5–40 million toman (approximately US$12–95 or €11–89) per child per year,[[256]](#cite_note-262) and 27% of children did not sign up for first grade because of the cost.[[257]](#cite_note-263)

#### Laos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Laos")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Lao_schoolgirls_reading_books.jpg/250px-Lao_schoolgirls_reading_books.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lao_schoolgirls_reading_books.jpg)

Three [Laotian](/wiki/Lao_people "Lao people") girls sit outside their school reading.

[Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") has the lowest level of adult literacy in all of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), other than [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor").[[258]](#cite_note-264)

Obstacles to literacy vary by country and culture, as writing systems, quality of education, availability of written material, competition from other sources (television, video games, cell phones, and family obligations), and culture all influence literacy levels. In Laos, which has a [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") alphabet, reading is relatively easy to learn—especially compared to [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), where spelling and pronunciation rules are filled with exceptions, and [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), with thousands of symbols to be memorized. However, a lack of books and other written materials has hindered functional literacy in Laos.[[259]](#cite_note-265) Many children and adults read so haltingly that the skill is hardly beneficial.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A literacy project in Laos addresses this by using what it calls "books that make literacy fun!" The project, [Big Brother Mouse](/wiki/Big_Brother_Mouse "Big Brother Mouse"), publishes colorful, easy-to-read books, then delivers them during book parties at rural schools. Some of the books are modeled on successful western books by authors such as [Dr. Seuss](/wiki/Dr._Seuss "Dr. Seuss"); the most popular, however, are traditional Laotian fairy tales. Two popular collections of folktales were written by Siphone Vouthisakdee, who comes from a village where only five children finished primary school.[[260]](#cite_note-266)

Big Brother Mouse has also created village reading rooms and published books for adult readers about subjects such as Buddhism, health, and baby care.[[261]](#cite_note-267)

#### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), the [National Commission for Human Development](/wiki/National_Commission_for_Human_Development "National Commission for Human Development") aims to bring literacy to adults, especially women. While speaking at a function held in connection with [International Literacy Day](/wiki/International_Literacy_Day "International Literacy Day"), Islamabad Director Kozue Kay Nagata said:

> Illiteracy in Pakistan has fallen over two decades, thanks to the government and people of Pakistan for their efforts working toward meeting the [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals"). Today, 70 percent of Pakistani youths can read and write. In 20 years, illiterate population has been reduced significantly.

She also emphasized the need to do more to improve literacy in the country, saying:

> The proportion of population in Pakistan lacking basic reading and writing is too high. This is a serious obstacle for individual fulfillment, to the development of societies, and to mutual understanding between peoples.

Referring to the recent national survey carried out by the Ministry of Education, Trainings and Standards in Higher Education with the support of UNESCO, [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), and provincial and area departments of education, Nagata pointed out that in Pakistan, although 70% of children finish primary school, a gender gap still exists as 68% of girls finish compared to 71% of boys.

Referring specifically to [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), she said that while the primary school completion rate is higher at 76%, there is a gender gap of 8 percentage points: 72% of girls compared to 80% for boys. She also noted that the average cost per primary school student (ages five–nine) was higher in Punjab at Rs 6,998 (approximately US$24 or €22.5).

In [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan"), although almost the same amount (Rs 6,985) is spent per child as in Punjab, the primary school completion rate is only 53%: 54% for girls and 52% for boys.

The Literate Pakistan Foundation, a non-profit organization established in 2003, is a case study bringing to light solutions for improving literacy rates in Pakistan. Their data shows that in [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), the primary school completion rate is 67%, which is lower than the national average of 70%. Furthermore, a gender gap exists, with only 65% of girls completing primary school compared to 68% of boys. In Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, the education expenditure per student at the primary school level (age five–nine) is Rs 8,638 ($30, €28).

In [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), the primary school completion rate is 63%, with a gender gap of 67% of girls completing primary school compared to 60% of boys.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, the education expenditure per student at the primary school level (age five–nine) is Rs 5,019 ($17.50, €16.50).

Nagata, referencing the report, said that the most common reason for children ages 10–18 (both boys and girls) leaving school is "the child [is] not willing to go to school", which may be related to quality and learning outcome. She added that the second-highest reason for girls living in rural communities dropping out is that their "parents did not allow" them to continue school, which might be related to prejudice and cultural norms surrounding girls.

#### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main article: [Baybayin](/wiki/Baybayin "Baybayin")

About 91.6% of Filipinos ages 10–64 were functionally literate in 2019, according to the results of the 2019 Functional Literacy, Education and Mass Media Survey; this translates to around 73.0 million out of the population of 79.7 million.[[248]](#cite_note-Functional-Literacy-Rate-Philippine-Statistics-Authority-254)
Starting in 300 BCE, early Filipinos devised and used their own writing system derived from the Brahmic family of scripts of [ancient India](/wiki/Ancient_India "Ancient India"). [Baybayin](/wiki/Baybayin "Baybayin") became the most widespread of these derived scripts by the 11th century. Early chroniclers, who came during the first Spanish expeditions to the islands, noted the proficiency of some of the natives, especially the chieftain and local kings, in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Old Javanese](/wiki/Old_Javanese "Old Javanese"), [Old Malay](/wiki/Old_Malay "Old Malay"), and several other languages.[[262]](#cite_note-268)[[263]](#cite_note-269)

During the Spanish colonization of the islands, reading materials were destroyed far less than during the Spanish colonization of the Americas. Education and literacy were introduced solely to the [Peninsulares](/wiki/Peninsulares "Peninsulares") and remained a privilege until the arrival of Americans, who introduced a public school system to the country, and English became the *lingua franca* in the Philippines. During the brief [Japanese occupation of the Philippines](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_the_Philippines "Japanese occupation of the Philippines"), the Japanese were able to teach their language and teach the children their written language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

Main article: [Education in Sri Lanka](/wiki/Education_in_Sri_Lanka "Education in Sri Lanka")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Sarachchandra_Theatre.jpg/250px-Sarachchandra_Theatre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarachchandra_Theatre.jpg)

The [University of Peradeniya](/wiki/University_of_Peradeniya "University of Peradeniya")'s [Sarachchandra open-air theatre](/wiki/Sarachchandra_open-air_theatre "Sarachchandra open-air theatre"), named in memory of [Ediriweera Sarachchandra](/wiki/Ediriweera_Sarachchandra "Ediriweera Sarachchandra"), Sri Lanka's premier playwright

With a [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") of 92.5%,[[249]](#cite_note-Ministry-of-Finance-Sri-Lanka-2011-255) Sri Lanka has one of the most literate [populations](/wiki/Populations "Populations") among [developing nations](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country").[[264]](#cite_note-270) Its youth literacy rate stands at 98%,[[250]](#cite_note-indexmundicom-256) its computer literacy rate at 35%,[[265]](#cite_note-271) and its primary school enrollment rate at over 99%.[[266]](#cite_note-272) An education system that dictates nine years of [compulsory schooling](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education") for every child is in place. The [free education system](/wiki/Free_education "Free education"), established in 1945,[[267]](#cite_note-273) is a result of the initiative of [C. W. W. Kannangara](/wiki/C._W._W._Kannangara "C. W. W. Kannangara") and A. Ratnayake.[[268]](#cite_note-274)[[269]](#cite_note-275) Sri Lanka is one of the few countries in the world that provides universal free education from the primary to the tertiary stage.[[270]](#cite_note-276)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Oceania")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Literacy&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Australia")]

A 2016–2017 survey of adult skills conducted by the Australian Bureau of Statistics on behalf of the OECD found that one in five adults of working age has low literacy skills, numeracy skills, or both.[[271]](#cite_note-277) The [Australian Early Development Census](/wiki/Australian_Early_Development_Census "Australian Early Development Census") National Report for 2021 reported that 82.6% of five-year-olds are on track to develop good language and cognitive skills.[[272]](#cite_note-278) In 2012–2013, Australia had 1515 public library service points, lending almost 174 million items to 10 million members at an average per capita cost of just under AU$45.[[273]](#cite_note-Said-2023-279) By 2020–2021, this had increased to a total of 1690 library outlets with just over 9 million registered or active members.[[273]](#cite_note-Said-2023-279)
